title: Feedback, both ways
description: How to give it without bruising — and take it without crumbling.
summaryPoints: Aim feedback at behavior, not character — 'the report was late twice', not 'you're unreliable'
Use the clean frame: situation, then behavior, then the impact on you
When you're on the receiving end, breathe first — defensiveness blocks growth
Separate the signal from the delivery: keep the useful part, drop the tone
Treat feedback as information, not a verdict on your worth

content.title: Two-way street
content.body:
Nobody loves feedback. Giving it feels like you might wreck a friendship; getting it feels like a punch you have to smile through.

But feedback is just **information** — about a project, a habit, a moment that didn't land. Handle it well in both directions and you become the person people actually want on their team.

This lesson is about both seats: the giving chair and the receiving one.
content.imageAlt: A balance scale weighing care and honesty

content.title: Behavior, not character
content.body:
The golden rule of giving feedback: aim at **what someone did**, not who they are.

- "The report was late twice this week" → fixable, specific, fair
- "You're so unreliable" → an attack on their character

The first one points at a behavior they can change. The second one labels them, and labels make people defend themselves instead of listen. Be specific, and stay on the behavior.

content.prompt: Good feedback should target the kind of person someone is, not just what they did.
content.explanation: Flip it — good feedback targets behavior, which is fixable. Labeling someone's character just makes them defensive.

content.prompt: Helpful feedback or harsh? Sort each line.

items.text: You interrupted me three times in that call
You never let anyone else talk
The slides were missing the numbers we needed
You're lazy and you don't care
Texting back a day late left me unsure if we were still on

content.title: The clean frame
content.body:
Here's a frame that works almost every time: **situation, behavior, impact.**

"In the meeting, when you cut me off, I felt dismissed."

- **Situation** — where it happened
- **Behavior** — the specific thing they did
- **Impact** — how it actually landed on you

Notice it's an I-statement, not a "you always" attack. You're sharing your experience, not handing down a verdict — so they can actually hear it.

content.prompt: Put the clean feedback frame in order.
steps: Name the situation: in yesterday's meeting
Name the behavior: when you cut me off
Name the impact: I felt dismissed
content.explanation: Situation sets the scene, behavior points at the fixable thing, and impact shows why it matters — without blaming.

content.title: Lead with care, pick your moment
content.body:
Two things make feedback land instead of sting:

- **Lead with care.** People can tell whether you're trying to help or trying to win. If they trust you want good for them, the hard part goes down easier.
- **Pick the moment.** Private, calm, not in front of the group, not when they're already fried. Same words, totally different result.

And make it a conversation, not a sentence handed down. Ask what they saw too.

content.prompt: When's the best time to give your friend honest feedback about the group project?
content.context: Your friend keeps turning in their part late and it's stressing the whole group.
options: In a calm, private moment, one-on-one
In the group chat so everyone backs you up
Right after they mess up, in front of the team
Never — just quietly do their part for them
content.explanation: Private and calm protects their dignity, so they can actually hear you instead of defending themselves in front of an audience.

content.instructions: One word breaks this rule. Tap it.
content.sentence: Good feedback is a public conversation, not a verdict handed down.
content.explanation: Feedback lands best one-on-one. Calling someone out in front of others triggers defensiveness, not change.

content.title: Now flip the chair
content.body:
Taking feedback is the harder seat. Your gut wants to defend, explain, fire back — and defensiveness slams the door on anything useful.

The move: **breathe** before you react. Assume good intent. Listen all the way through instead of building your comeback. Ask a clarifying question. Then say, "Thank you — let me think about that."

You don't have to decide on the spot. You just have to stay open long enough to hear it.

content.prompt: What's the most useful first response?
content.context: A teammate tells you your messages come across as kind of blunt.
options: "Can you give me an example so I get what you mean?"
"That's just how I talk, deal with it."
content.explanation: Asking for a specific example shows you're listening and gives you something concrete to work with — that's curiosity, not defense.

content.prompt: Swap each defensive move for the open one.
You just got tough feedback. Put your response in order.
content.explanation: Breathing buys you a beat, listening gets the full message, clarifying sharpens it — and you decide what's true once you've cooled off.

content.title: Signal vs. delivery
content.body:
Here's the freeing part: you don't have to accept all of it.

Sometimes feedback arrives wrapped in a bad mood or clumsy wording. **Separate the signal from the delivery** — take the useful core, let the harsh tone go.

And remember: feedback is information, not a verdict on your worth. One late report doesn't make you a failure. The people who grow fastest aren't the ones who never get criticized — they're the ones who actively ask for it.

content.prompt: What does "separate the signal from the delivery" mean?
content.context: Someone snaps, "This is sloppy, did you even try?" — and they're a little right about the typos.
options: Ignore the whole thing because they were rude
Accept that you're a sloppy person who didn't try
Keep the useful point (fix the typos), let the rude tone go
Snap back so they feel as bad as you do
content.explanation: The tone was harsh, but there's a real signal underneath. Take the fixable part and don't let the delivery convince you it's a verdict on you.

content.prompt: Build a piece of feedback that's kind AND specific.
content.context: Your group project partner turned their part in late twice, and it left you scrambling. You want to say something without blowing up the friendship.

options.text: When the project was turned in late twice

options.why: That labels their character. They'll defend themselves instead of hearing you.

options.text: You clearly never care about anything
options.why: An exaggeration and an attack — it's about who they are, not what happened.

options.text: I ended up stressed and scrambling at the end
you basically ruined the whole thing
options.why: Overstated and blaming — it makes them defensive instead of understanding.
Dragging in 'everyone' turns honest feedback into a pile-on.

options.text: could we set a quick check-in next time?
so just fix your attitude already
options.why: Vague and hostile — it gives them nothing to actually do.

content.explanation: Behavior, then impact, then a forward-looking ask — specific, owns your experience, and points at the fix instead of the person.
